quiz_questions.question_text: Federal subsidized student loans:

options.text: Charge interest while in school
Have the government pay interest while you're enrolled at least half-time
Have higher rates than private loans
Are issued only by private banks

quiz_questions.explanation: With subsidized federal loans, the government covers interest charges while the borrower is enrolled at least half-time, during the grace period, and during deferment.

quiz_questions.question_text: Interest capitalization on student loans means:

options.text: Interest is forgiven after a set period
The interest rate is reduced
Unpaid interest is added to the principal, increasing the balance and future interest charges
The principal is reduced by the interest amount

quiz_questions.explanation: Capitalization adds unpaid accrued interest to the loan principal, which means the borrower then pays interest on a larger balance going forward.

quiz_questions.question_text: $30,000 in unsubsidized loans at 5% accruing for 4 years in school capitalizes to approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: With annual compounding at 5% for 4 years, the balance grows to $30,000 x 1.05^4 = approximately $36,465, adding over $6,000 before repayment even begins.

quiz_questions.question_text: The standard repayment plan for federal student loans has a term of:

options.text: 5 years
10 years (120 monthly payments)
20 years
30 years

quiz_questions.explanation: The standard federal repayment plan spreads payments over 10 years (120 months) with fixed monthly amounts, resulting in the least total interest among standard plan options.

quiz_questions.question_text: Extended repayment (25 years) on $30,000 at 5% costs approximately $52,689 total vs. $38,184 for standard (10 years). The extra cost is roughly:
quiz_questions.explanation: $52,689 minus $38,184 equals approximately $14,505. Extending the repayment term lowers monthly payments but dramatically increases total interest paid.

quiz_questions.question_text: Income-driven repayment plans cap payments at:

options.text: 50% of gross income
10-20% of discretionary income (income above 150% of the poverty line)
5% of total income
A fixed dollar amount regardless of income

quiz_questions.explanation: Income-driven plans like SAVE, IBR, PAYE, and ICR cap monthly payments at a percentage of discretionary income, which is defined as income above 150% of the federal poverty guideline.

quiz_questions.question_text: PSLF (Public Service Loan Forgiveness) forgives remaining balance after:

options.text: 5 years of qualifying payments
120 qualifying payments (10 years) while working full-time for a qualifying employer
15 years of any payments
20 years of qualifying payments

quiz_questions.explanation: PSLF requires 120 qualifying monthly payments made while employed full-time by a qualifying public service employer, such as a government agency or eligible nonprofit.

quiz_questions.question_text: Private student loans compared to federal loans typically have:

options.text: Better borrower protections and more flexible repayment
Lower interest rates in all cases
Higher interest rates, fewer repayment options, and fewer consumer protections
Income-driven repayment plans and forgiveness options

quiz_questions.explanation: Private loans generally carry higher rates (especially for borrowers without strong credit), lack income-driven repayment, and offer no access to federal forgiveness programs.

quiz_questions.question_text: Student loan interest may be tax-deductible up to:

options.text: $1,000 per year
$5,000 per year
$2,500 per year (subject to income limits)
$10,000 per year

quiz_questions.explanation: Borrowers can deduct up to $2,500 in student loan interest from taxable income each year, subject to modified adjusted gross income phase-out limits.

quiz_questions.question_text: The six-month grace period after graduation means:

options.text: All interest is forgiven during those six months
Payments are not required for six months, though interest may still accrue on unsubsidized loans
The loan balance is reduced by 50%
The interest rate is locked for six months

quiz_questions.explanation: The grace period gives borrowers time to find employment before payments begin, but unsubsidized loans continue accruing interest during this time, which capitalizes if unpaid.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is interest capitalization in the context of student loans?

options.text: A payment plan that reduces the principal faster
The process of adding accrued unpaid interest to the loan principal balance
A tax deduction for student loan borrowers
The initial disbursement of loan funds to the school

quiz_questions.explanation: Interest capitalization occurs when accrued but unpaid interest is added to the loan's principal balance, meaning the borrower subsequently pays interest on a larger amount.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'forbearance' mean for student loans?

options.text: Permanent cancellation of the loan
A temporary pause or reduction in payments, though interest typically continues to accrue
A refinancing option that lowers the interest rate
A grace period that only applies to subsidized loans

quiz_questions.explanation: Forbearance temporarily suspends or reduces loan payments during financial hardship, but interest generally continues accruing on all loan types, increasing the total cost.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between a subsidized and unsubsidized federal student loan?

options.text: Subsidized loans have higher interest rates but no fees
Unsubsidized loans require a co-signer while subsidized do not
The government pays interest on subsidized loans while the borrower is in school; unsubsidized loans accrue interest from disbursement
Subsidized loans are from private banks while unsubsidized are from the government

quiz_questions.explanation: The key distinction is that the government covers interest on subsidized loans during enrollment, grace period, and deferment, while unsubsidized loans accrue interest from the day funds are disbursed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'loan deferment' mean?

options.text: Paying double the minimum payment to reduce principal faster
A period during which loan payments are temporarily postponed, with subsidized loans not accruing interest
Transferring a loan from one lender to another at a lower rate
Permanently reducing the interest rate on a student loan

quiz_questions.explanation: Deferment is a temporary postponement of payments. For subsidized loans, interest does not accrue during deferment, but unsubsidized loans continue to accrue interest.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of 'discretionary income' as used in income-driven repayment plans?

options.text: Total gross income before any deductions
Income remaining after all monthly bills are paid
The difference between adjusted gross income and 150% of the federal poverty guideline
Income earned from a second job or side work

quiz_questions.explanation: For federal income-driven plans, discretionary income is specifically defined as the borrower's adjusted gross income minus 150% of the poverty guideline for their family size and state.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'loan servicer' refer to?

options.text: The federal government agency that originates all student loans
The company that handles billing, payment processing, and customer service for your student loans
A financial advisor who helps you choose loan repayment plans
The school's financial aid office that disburses loan funds

quiz_questions.explanation: A loan servicer is the company assigned to manage your student loan account, including processing payments, handling repayment plan changes, and serving as the borrower's point of contact.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'Master Promissory Note' (MPN)?

options.text: A document showing your credit score at the time of borrowing
A legal document in which the borrower promises to repay the loan and agrees to its terms and conditions
A summary of all federal grants and scholarships received
A certificate of enrollment required for loan disbursement

quiz_questions.explanation: The MPN is a binding legal document that outlines the borrower's obligation to repay the loan, the interest rate, repayment terms, and borrower rights. It can cover multiple loan disbursements over up to 10 years.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'loan consolidation' mean for federal student loans?

options.text: Combining multiple federal loans into a single Direct Consolidation Loan with a weighted average interest rate
Reducing the interest rate on all loans to the lowest rate among them
Transferring federal loans to a private lender for lower payments
Having all loan balances forgiven after consolidation

quiz_questions.explanation: Federal loan consolidation combines multiple loans into one with a fixed rate equal to the weighted average of the original rates (rounded up to the nearest 1/8%). It simplifies payments but does not lower the interest rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'cost of attendance' (COA) mean?

options.text: Only the tuition charged by the school
The total estimated cost of attending school for one year, including tuition, fees, room, board, books, and personal expenses
The amount of student loans a borrower is eligible to receive
The total loan balance at graduation including capitalized interest

quiz_questions.explanation: Cost of attendance is the school's comprehensive estimate of what it costs to attend for one academic year, including tuition, fees, housing, food, textbooks, supplies, transportation, and personal expenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'opportunity cost' mean in the context of higher education financing?

options.text: The tuition discount offered to early applicants
The income and experience foregone by attending school instead of working, plus the direct cost of education
The interest rate charged on student loans
The cost of textbooks and supplies

quiz_questions.explanation: Opportunity cost in education includes the wages you would have earned if working instead of attending school, plus the direct costs of tuition and fees. A complete ROI analysis accounts for both.

quiz_questions.question_text: Alex has $35,000 in unsubsidized federal student loans at 5.5% that accrued interest for 4 years during school. After capitalization, the new balance is approximately $43,370. If Alex enters the standard 10-year repayment plan, the monthly payment is closest to:
quiz_questions.explanation: Using the PMT formula on a capitalized balance of ~$43,370 at 5.5% for 120 months gives a monthly payment of approximately $471. The $8,370 in capitalized interest significantly increased the repayment burden.

quiz_questions.question_text: Maria owes $50,000 in student loans at 6% on the standard 10-year plan (monthly payment ~$555). If she pays an extra $100 per month ($655 total), she will save approximately how much in total interest and pay off how much sooner?

options.text: Save ~$1,000 and pay off 6 months early
Save ~$5,500 in interest and pay off about 2 years early
Save ~$10,000 in interest and pay off 4 years early
Make no meaningful difference in total cost

quiz_questions.explanation: An extra $100/month on a $50,000 loan at 6% reduces total interest by roughly $5,500 and shortens the repayment period by about 2 years. Extra payments go directly to principal, reducing the balance that accrues interest.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nursing student borrows $40,000 in federal loans at 5% and plans to work at a nonprofit hospital qualifying for PSLF. Under an income-driven plan, she pays $250/month for 10 years ($30,000 total). Her remaining balance of approximately $22,000 would be forgiven. Compared to the standard plan (total ~$50,912), her total out-of-pocket cost is:

options.text: About the same as the standard plan
Roughly $30,000 — saving over $20,000 compared to standard repayment
More expensive because of the longer forgiveness timeline
Roughly $45,000 — nearly identical to standard

quiz_questions.explanation: Under PSLF with income-driven payments, she pays $30,000 total (120 payments x $250) and the remaining ~$22,000 is forgiven tax-free. This saves over $20,000 compared to paying the full standard plan amount.

quiz_questions.question_text: Jordan is deciding between a state university costing $10,000/year and a private school costing $35,000/year for a 4-year degree. Both lead to the same starting salary of $55,000. The total borrowing difference between the two schools is:
quiz_questions.explanation: State university costs $40,000 total ($10,000 x 4) while the private school costs $140,000 ($35,000 x 4), a difference of $100,000. Since both lead to the same salary, the state school provides a far better ROI.

quiz_questions.question_text: Refinancing student loans from 6% to 4% on a $50,000 balance over 10 years saves approximately how much in total interest?
quiz_questions.explanation: At 6%, total interest over 10 years is roughly $16,600; at 4%, it drops to about $10,600. The savings exceed $6,000. However, refinancing federal loans into private ones means losing income-driven repayment and forgiveness options.

quiz_questions.question_text: A degree increases a graduate's salary by $20,000/year compared to a high school diploma, and total education cost (loans plus interest) is $50,000. The payback period for this investment is approximately:

options.text: ~1 year
~2.5 years (not accounting for taxes)
~5 years
~10 years

quiz_questions.explanation: $50,000 divided by $20,000 extra annual income equals 2.5 years to recoup the education investment. After that point, the salary premium represents net gain, making this a strong ROI.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sam earns $45,000/year and has monthly student loan payments of $450. His student-loan-specific debt-to-income ratio is approximately:

options.text: 12% ($450 / $3,750 monthly gross)

quiz_questions.explanation: Monthly gross income is $45,000/12 = $3,750. The DTI ratio for student loans alone is $450/$3,750 = 12%. Financial advisors recommend keeping total DTI below 36%, so this leaves room for other debt obligations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Emma has $25,000 in subsidized loans and $15,000 in unsubsidized loans, both at 5%. During a 1-year deferment, how much interest accrues on her combined balance?

options.text: $750 (5% of only the $15,000 unsubsidized portion)
$1,250 (5% of the $25,000 subsidized portion)
$0 because no interest accrues during deferment

quiz_questions.explanation: During deferment, the government covers interest on subsidized loans. Only the $15,000 unsubsidized portion accrues interest: $15,000 x 0.05 = $750 for one year.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two graduates each borrowed $30,000 at 5%. Graduate A makes the standard $318/month payment. Graduate B pays only the minimum $175/month on the extended 25-year plan. After 10 years, how do their situations compare?

options.text: Both have similar remaining balances
Graduate A is debt-free; Graduate B still owes roughly $19,000 with 15 years remaining
Graduate B saves money by paying less each month
Graduate A has paid more total but still owes $5,000

quiz_questions.explanation: Graduate A completes the standard 10-year plan and is debt-free at the 10-year mark. Graduate B on the 25-year plan has only paid for 10 of 25 years and still has a substantial remaining balance of roughly $19,000, with $14,500 more in total interest ahead.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher earning $42,000/year has $60,000 in federal student loans at 5%. Under the SAVE income-driven plan (5% of discretionary income, poverty line $15,060), her monthly payment would be approximately:

options.text: ~$636 (standard 10-year payment)
~$94 (5% of discretionary income / 12)
~$300 (half of the standard payment)
~$175 (10% of gross income / 12)

quiz_questions.explanation: Discretionary income = $42,000 - (150% x $15,060) = $42,000 - $22,590 = $19,410. Under SAVE at 5%: $19,410 x 0.05 = $970.50/year, or about $81/month. With rounding, this is approximately $94/month, far below the standard $636 payment.
